summary.intent: Address a second round of Codex review findings on PR #681 in the ars-ui repo, specifically fixing CJK day-period edge cases, label/literal attribute issues in the TimeField component, and keeping the spec in sync with the implementation.
summary.outcome: All five new Codex review threads were resolved: CJK day-period ASCII fallback filtering, invalid `for` attribute removed from label attrs, literal segment index fallback corrected, snapshots refreshed, and a clean commit pushed with the Codex co-author trailer. Full CI suite (formatting, clippy, unit tests, spec conformance, proptests) passed green.

repo: Cargo does not accept multiple test name filters in a single `cargo test` invocation; use module-level filtering instead.
Snapshot updates require `cargo insta accept` after intentional behavioral changes; pending snapshots block CI.
The repo uses `cargo xtask spec validate` to ensure spec markdown stays in sync with implementation.
Codex review is triggered via a comment on the PR issue and polled via GitHub GraphQL API for unresolved threads authored by 'codex'.
The `snapshot-reviewed` label is used to gate merge state alongside CI checks.
Mutation testing reruns are expensive and can be skipped for focused review passes when Codex findings are narrow in scope.

code.finding: Label attrs generation was incorrectly emitting a `for` attribute targeting a non-labelable field group element; fix relies on `aria-labelledby` on the group instead.
CJK day-period typing logic now filters to only buffer valid CJK prefixes while still allowing backend ASCII shortcuts; invalid prefixes are discarded rather than passed through.
Literal segment index fallback was not using the actual segment index; corrected to reference the real position in the segment list.
Label snapshot required manual refresh after removing the invalid `for` attribute; `cargo insta accept` without a path argument accepts all pending snapshots across the workspace.
Spec text and examples for CJK day-period typing, label attrs, and literal fallback were updated to match the reviewed implementation; spec was ahead of old behavior and needed realignment.

workflow: Poll loop pattern: check reactions, unresolved Codex threads via GraphQL, and PR CI status in parallel every 120 seconds to detect when Codex posts new findings.
Run targeted unit tests first before touching production code to anchor changes to exact reported regressions.
Format check (`cargo +nightly fmt --all --check`), clippy, unit tests, spec conformance, and proptests all run as a local verification suite before committing.
Commits to resolve Codex review require the `Co-authored-by: OpenAI Codex <codex@openai.com>` trailer.
Resolving review threads and replying to them are separate GitHub API operations that must both be performed.
Mutation test reruns can be explicitly deferred when review findings are narrow to avoid long CI cycles.

friction: Cargo rejects multiple test name filters in a single invocation, requiring a fallback to module-level filtering which runs more tests than needed.
`cargo insta accept` requires either a package flag or a path argument; the bare command without flags accepted all workspace snapshots which may be broader than intended.
Polling loop requires repeated 120-second sleeps between CI status checks with no event-driven alternative, making the feedback cycle slow.
Five new Codex findings arrived mid-CI-run, requiring a context switch back to code changes before the previous CI run had fully completed.
open_items: Mutation testing was explicitly skipped for this review pass and should be re-run before final merge to ensure the CJK day-period and literal index changes have adequate mutation coverage.
The blur/focus CJK fallback regression test was noted as already covered by existing behavior but warrants a follow-up review to confirm it exercises the new filtering path specifically.
Codex review cycle was still in progress (👀 state) at session end; a subsequent poll is needed to confirm approval or catch any additional findings on commit `24c1d47c`.
